briefs.title: Burnaby shelter at 3020 Gilmore cannot open until Good Neighbour and Operational Plans approved
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council voted to block occupancy of the new diversion shelter at 3020 Gilmore Avenue until BC Housing and the shelter operator submit two plans — a Good Neighbour Plan (a written commitment on how the facility will work with the surrounding community) and an Operational Management Plan (a document outlining day-to-day rules and procedures) — and the City approves both. Council also asked BC Housing to run a broader public consultation before the shelter opens.
briefs.action_required: If your business is near 3020 Gilmore, watch for BC Housing's public re-engagement process and participate.
briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Professional Services

briefs.title: Burnaby rezones 8265–8303 10th Avenue — land use rules at this address have changed
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council has given final approval to Bylaw #14780, which changes the zoning (the official rules about how land can be used or developed) for properties at 8265–8303 10th Avenue. Any business owner, landlord, or developer with an interest in this specific block should be aware that the permitted uses and development rules for these properties have now officially changed.
briefs.action_required: If you own, lease, or plan to develop property at 8265–8303 10th Avenue, contact Burnaby Planning to confirm what is now permitted.

briefs.title: Burnaby updates its Procedure Bylaw — how Council meetings are run is changing
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council passed all three readings of Amendment Bylaw No. 1, 2026 to the Burnaby Procedure Bylaw 2024. This update changes the internal rules that govern how Council meetings are run — such as how motions are made, how votes are conducted, and how public meetings are organized.

briefs.title: Burnaby Plumbing Permit Fees Updated — What You'll Pay Has Changed
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council has officially passed an amendment to its Consolidated Fees and Charges Bylaw, updating the fees charged for plumbing permits. If you plan to do any plumbing work that requires a permit in Burnaby, the cost to get that permit has changed.
briefs.action_required: Check the new Burnaby plumbing permit fee schedule before budgeting your next plumbing project.

briefs.title: Burnaby Bylaw Updated to Regulate Bear Spray Under Notice Enforcement Rules
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council passed all three readings of Bylaw #14799, which amends the Burnaby Bylaw Notice Enforcement Bylaw 2009 to add rules around bear spray. This means bear spray is now subject to the city's bylaw notice enforcement system (a process that lets the city issue fines or penalties for rule violations without going to court).
briefs.action_required: Check if your business sells or allows bear spray on-site and review Burnaby's updated bylaw rules.

briefs.title: Burnaby now regulates bear spray sales under its Business Licence Bylaw
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council passed all three readings of Bylaw #14798, which adds rules about selling bear spray to the Burnaby Business Licence Bylaw 2017. Businesses in Burnaby that sell bear spray will now need to follow these new licensing requirements.
briefs.action_required: Check if your Burnaby business sells bear spray and confirm you meet the new licence requirements.
briefs.industries: Retail, Grocery & Food Retail, Natural Resources

briefs.title: Burnaby North Road Business Improvement Area Bylaw 2026 passes all three readings
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council has passed all three readings of the Burnaby North Road Business Improvement Area (BIA) Bylaw 2026. A BIA is a defined district where local businesses collectively fund and manage improvements to the area — such as marketing, events, and streetscape upgrades — through a levy (an extra charge) added to their property tax bill.
briefs.action_required: Check if your business is within the Burnaby North Road BIA boundary and find out what levy you will owe.

briefs.title: Burnaby rezones 7305 & 7317 Buller Ave — first and second reading passed
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council has passed the first and second reading of Bylaw #14794, which proposes to rezone (change the permitted land use) for two properties at 7305 and 7317 Buller Avenue. The bylaw must still complete further readings and approval before the rezoning is final.
briefs.action_required: Monitor Burnaby Council agendas for the public hearing and final reading dates if this rezoning affects your business or property.

briefs.title: Burnaby Zoning Bylaw text amendment passes first and second reading
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council has given first and second reading to Bylaw No. 14792, which makes a text amendment (a written change to the rules) to the Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 1965. The bylaw must still pass third reading and adoption before it becomes law, so the full details of what land-use rules are changing are not yet confirmed.
briefs.action_required: Monitor Burnaby Council agendas for third reading and adoption of Bylaw No. 14792.
briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail

briefs.title: Stoney Creek watershed development approvals may be paused pending staff review
briefs.summary: City council has sent a request from the Stoney Creek Environment Committee to staff for review. The committee asked the city to create new protections for the Stoney Creek watershed (the land and waterways that drain into Stoney Creek) and to temporarily stop approving new development in that area until those protections are in place.
briefs.action_required: If you plan to develop near Stoney Creek, monitor city staff reports for a potential development approval pause.

briefs.title: Stoney Creek construction impact rules under feasibility review — no changes yet
briefs.summary: City council has sent recommendations from the Environment Committee about reducing the impact of construction near Stoney Creek to staff for a feasibility review. Staff will study the proposals and report back before any new rules or requirements are put in place.

briefs.title: City approves 2026 active transportation education and promotion initiatives
briefs.summary: City council has approved a set of education and promotion initiatives for active transportation (walking, cycling, and other non-motorized travel) for 2026, based on a Transportation Committee report from February 11, 2026. These programs are designed to encourage residents and commuters to use active travel options more often.

briefs.title: 49-Storey Mixed-Use Tower at Springer Avenue Moves Forward After First Reading
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council has approved the first reading of a rezoning bylaw (a rule that changes how land can be used) for a 49-storey residential and retail tower at 2209 & 2225 Springer Avenue. The approval includes a Housing Agreement and a preliminary green light to sell City-owned land to make the project possible.
briefs.action_required: If your business operates near Springer Avenue, monitor future council readings for construction and zoning impacts.
briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail

briefs.title: Burnaby Lake Village rezoning bylaw advances: taller buildings and revised open space rules proposed
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council has moved forward a bylaw to update the Burnaby Lake Village Master Plan at 6800 Lougheed Highway. The proposed changes allow for taller and larger buildings and revised open space requirements, in line with the city's long-term planning document, OCP 2050 (the Official Community Plan, which guides how land in Burnaby is used and developed).
briefs.action_required: If your business operates near 6800 Lougheed Highway, monitor Burnaby Council meetings for the public hearing date.
briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail

briefs.title: 20-storey rental tower at 5058 Imperial Street replaces approved 6-storey plan
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council has approved moving forward with a rezoning bylaw (a rule that changes how land can be used) for a 20-storey mixed-use market rental building at 5058 Imperial Street. This replaces a previously approved 6-storey plan and is now advancing to First and Second Reading — the early formal steps before a bylaw becomes law.
briefs.action_required: If your business or property is near 5058 Imperial Street, monitor upcoming public hearings for this rezoning.
briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Tenant

briefs.title: City-owned EV charging stations switch to energy-based fees; three bylaws amended
briefs.summary: A city council has approved a new way to charge drivers for using public electric vehicle (EV) charging stations — instead of paying by time, users will now pay based on the amount of energy (electricity) they use. Three city bylaws are being updated to reflect this change, covering parking and EV charging rules, fees and charges, and bylaw enforcement.
briefs.action_required: If your business uses or manages city EV chargers, review the new energy-based fee structure when it takes effect.

briefs.title: On-street public pay parking fees increased by city council
briefs.summary: City council has approved an increase to the fees drivers pay at on-street public parking meters. The new rates were outlined in a Financial Management Committee report from February 17, 2026. If your business relies on customer street parking, your customers will pay more to park nearby.
briefs.action_required: Check the new parking rates and let customers know costs have increased for on-street parking near your business.

briefs.title: Heritage Alteration Permit granted for chimney work at Burnaby's W.J. Mathers House
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council approved a Heritage Alteration Permit (official permission required before making changes to a protected historic building) for chimney alterations at the W.J. Mathers House 'Altnadene' at 6490 Deer Lake Avenue. This means the proposed chimney work at this heritage-designated property is now authorized to proceed.

briefs.title: City renews $670,000 Guardteck security contract for municipal sites and events
briefs.summary: City council approved a one-year contract renewal with Guardteck Security Corp to provide security officers at city-owned locations and special events. The total estimated cost is $670,000 including taxes.

briefs.title: City renews $162,400 portable toilet contract with Jiffy John Rentals
briefs.summary: Council approved a contract renewal with Jiffy John Rentals Ltd. to supply portable rental toilets at an estimated cost of $162,400 including taxes. This is an existing supplier relationship being extended — no new rules or compliance requirements are introduced for local businesses.

briefs.title: Up to 84 outdoor events approved at Deer Lake, Central Park, and Swangard Stadium in 2026
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council has approved noise bylaw exemptions for up to 84 outdoor events at Deer Lake Park, Central Park, and Swangard Stadium. These events can take place between March 15 and November 1, 2026, meaning organizers and nearby businesses can expect increased activity at these venues during that period.
briefs.action_required: If you operate near these venues, plan for increased foot traffic and noise between March 15 and November 1, 2026.

key_dates.label: Burnaby outdoor event season begins — Deer Lake, Central Park, Swangard Stadium
Burnaby outdoor event season ends — Deer Lake, Central Park, Swangard Stadium

briefs.title: Burnaby Council approves Option B design for Landmark Burnaby Sign project
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council has approved Option B as the chosen design for the Landmark Burnaby Sign initiative, directing staff to move forward with the project as described in the February 24, 2026 report. This is a municipal infrastructure and branding decision with no direct compliance requirements for most businesses.

briefs.title: Burnaby freezes $63,005 FCM membership dues until city gets Big City Mayors' Caucus seat
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council has paused payment of its 2026–2027 membership fees to the Federation of Canadian Municipalities (FCM), a national organization that represents cities and towns, until Burnaby is given a seat on the Big City Mayors' Caucus — a group that gives large cities a direct voice in federal policy discussions. The dues total $63,005.33 and will remain unpaid until that condition is met.

briefs.title: Commercial fleet parking and rentals permitted to continue at 7000 Lougheed Highway
briefs.summary: Council has renewed Temporary Use Permit #26-01 (a short-term approval that allows a property to be used in a way not normally permitted under standard zoning rules) for 7000 Lougheed Highway. The renewal allows commercial fleet vehicle parking and rental operations to keep running at that location.

briefs.title: Antenna variance application at 9001 University Crescent sent back for further staff review
briefs.summary: A development variance permit (a formal approval to build something that doesn't fully meet standard zoning rules) for antenna equipment at 9001 University Crescent has been sent back to staff for more review. The application sought exceptions to rules about how much of a building's face the antennas could cover and how tall they could be.

briefs.title: Antenna variance application at 8888 University Drive sent back for further staff review
briefs.summary: A permit application (a formal request for permission to build outside normal rules) to allow antenna equipment to exceed standard height and wall coverage limits at 8888 University Drive has been sent back to city staff for more review. No decision has been made yet.

briefs.title: Burnaby grants utility access rights over 3860 Sperling Avenue for Burnaby Lake Recreation Centre
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council approved a Statutory Right-of-Way (a legal permission allowing a third party to use a strip of land for a specific purpose) over part of 3860 Sperling Avenue. This lets utility providers install infrastructure needed to service the new Burnaby Lake Recreation Centre.

briefs.title: Construction permits in Lower Westridge may be paused pending hazard assessment
briefs.summary: Council has asked the BC Minister of Housing and Municipal Affairs to put a temporary stop (moratorium) on new construction permits in the Lower Westridge area. No new permits would be issued until a hazard assessment — a formal review of safety risks in the area — is finished.
briefs.action_required: If you plan to apply for a construction permit in Lower Westridge, hold off until the hazard assessment is complete.

briefs.title: City launches Natural Hazard Permit program; $120,000 approved to assess Lower Westridge for housing exemption
briefs.summary: City council has started a city-wide Natural Hazard Development Permit Area program, which means certain properties in hazard-prone areas may face extra rules before development is allowed. Council also approved $120,000 to study whether the Lower Westridge neighbourhood is too hazardous to be required to allow small-scale multi-unit housing (like duplexes and triplexes) under provincial mandates.
briefs.action_required: If you own or develop property in Lower Westridge, monitor the hazard assessment results for impacts on your building rights.

briefs.title: Burnaby now requires a special business licence condition to sell bear spray at retail
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council has amended its Business Licence Bylaw and Bylaw Notice Enforcement Bylaw to regulate the retail sale of bear spray. Stores that sell bear spray in Burnaby must now meet new rules tied to their business licence (the permit that allows them to legally operate).
briefs.action_required: Check with the City of Burnaby to confirm what licence conditions apply to your bear spray sales.

briefs.title: Burnaby North Road BIA renewed for 2026–2031 with new bylaw coming
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council has approved a five-year renewal of the North Road Business Improvement Area (BIA — a defined zone where local businesses collectively fund area improvements) for 2026–2031. The City Solicitor has been directed to draft the new Business Improvement Area Bylaw 2026, which will formalize the renewal.
briefs.action_required: Check if your business is within the North Road BIA zone and review the new bylaw when it is released.

briefs.title: Burnaby plumbing permit fees updated — check new costs before your next project
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council has approved small changes to the fees charged for plumbing permits under the city's Consolidated Fees and Charges Bylaw. If you plan to do any plumbing work that requires a permit in Burnaby, the cost of that permit may be slightly different than before.
briefs.action_required: Check Burnaby's updated plumbing permit fee schedule before applying for your next permit.

briefs.title: City will now remove outdated land-use restrictions from private properties on request
briefs.summary: City Council approved the removal of old restrictive covenants (legally binding rules attached to a property's title that limit how it can be used) that the City itself had placed on private properties. Property owners can now ask the City Solicitor to formally remove these outdated restrictions from their title.
briefs.action_required: If your property has a City-imposed restrictive covenant on its title, contact the City Solicitor's office to request its removal.
briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail
